summary:
The Wilton Industry was a Later Stone Age microlithic hunter-gatherer tradition of southern Africa, dated approximately 8000–2000 BCE, and one of the most widespread and long-lived Later Stone Age cultural traditions on the continent. Named after the type site of Wilton in the Eastern Cape of South Africa, the tradition is defined by its characteristic small geometric microlithic tools — lunates, segments, and other backed forms — made from silcrete, quartz, and other local stone materials.

Wilton communities were mobile hunter-gatherers who exploited the diverse environments of southern Africa, from the Kalahari semi-desert to coastal fynbos and eastern savanna. Their material culture, beyond stone tools, included worked bone, shell ornaments, and the production of rock art — the long tradition of San (Bushmen) rock painting in southern Africa, depicting eland, human figures, and shamanic transformations, has its roots in the traditions of Wilton and related Later Stone Age communities. The Wilton Industry demonstrates the continuity and adaptability of indigenous southern African hunter-gatherer traditions through a long period of environmental change and, eventually, contact with incoming Iron Age farming communities from the north.
